figures.caption: Fig. 8: LAPIS-SHRED backward inference on the 2D NDSI dataset from a single terminal frame using static padding. Ground truth (top row), LAPIS-SHRED reconstruction (second row), and SHRED baseline using the full sensor timeseries (third row), shown at selected time steps.

Fig. 7: LAPIS-SHRED forward inference on the 2D NDSI dataset. Ground truth for the 2025 snow season (top row), LAPIS-SHRED prediction from the initial 5 frames (second row), and SHRED baseline using the full sensor time-series (third row), shown at selected time steps.

Fig. 14: Distribution of relative reconstruction errors on the fluid-around-an-obstacle benchmark (reproduced from Tomasetto et al. [22], Figure 7b). SHRED-ROM outperforms all competing methods on the reconstruction task alone; the temporal inference setting addressed by LAPIS-SHRED introduces additional challenges that none of these methods are designed to handle.

Fig. 13: SHRED-ROM baseline (ensemble-interpolated) on the NDSI forward experiment. Ground truth (top), reconstruction from ensemble-interpolated sensor series (middle), and SHRED-ROM with full sensor time-series access (bottom). Compare with Figure 7.

Fig. 6: LAPIS-SHRED backward reconstruction on the 1D RDE ignition stage. Ground truth (top row), LAPIS-SHRED reconstruction from the terminal 20% of the trajectory (second row), and SHRED baseline using the full sensor history (third row), shown at selected time steps. Pressure field is shown in red; temperature in blue.

Fig. 15: LAPIS-SHRED backward reconstruction on 2D Kolmogorov flow: velocity field. Ground truth (top row), LAPIS-SHRED reconstruction from the terminal 10% of the trajectory (second row), and SHRED baseline using the full sensor time-series (third row), shown at selected time steps.
TABLE 3: Capability comparison of LAPIS-SHRED and related methods. ✓ = supported; ✗ = not supported.
Fig. 17: NDSI data preview: spatiotemporal structure of the 2020-2025 snow melt seasons after SCAF trimming.
